home.subtitle: Understand the five phases of a cyberattack.

mascot_script.text: Hi! I'm Neko, your guide through the Cyber Kill Chain, nyaa~!
I'll walk you through seven short lessons, then a short quiz. Hit Begin Learning whenever you're ready!

lessons.label: The Framework
lessons.title: Every cyberattack tells the same story.

mascot_script.text: Welcome to lesson 1! The Cyber Kill Chain was developed by Lockheed Martin as a map of how every cyberattack moves forward.
Every breach, big or small, typically to complete each of these 5 phases to succeed.
Click any circle below to preview that phase.

intro_bullets: Developed by Lockheed Martin (2011) as a defensive framework.
Describes 5 sequential phases every attack must complete.
Breaking any single phase stops the entire attack.

chain_nodes.label: Recon
chain_nodes.name: Reconnaissance
chain_nodes.desc: The attacker researches the target, scanning networks, harvesting emails, and mapping out the organization's attack surface.

chain_nodes.label: Arm & Deliver
chain_nodes.name: Arm & Deliver
chain_nodes.desc: The attacker crafts a tailored weapon (malware or a malicious document) and delivers it via phishing or compromised channels.

chain_nodes.label: Exploit
chain_nodes.name: Exploitation
chain_nodes.desc: The weapon activates: a vulnerability is triggered, code executes, and the attacker gains their initial foothold.

chain_nodes.label: Control
chain_nodes.name: Establish Control
chain_nodes.desc: The attacker installs persistent backdoors and establishes an encrypted command channel for ongoing remote access.

chain_nodes.label: Exfiltrate
chain_nodes.name: Exfiltration
chain_nodes.desc: The endgame: sensitive data is located, compressed, and secretly transferred to the attacker's external servers.

mascot_script.text: Phase 1 · Reconnaissance. The attacker has picked ACME Corp as their target.
This is PASSIVE recon — just Google, LinkedIn, job posts, public leaks. The victim never notices a thing.
Hit Start Scan to watch them harvest employee emails from LinkedIn!
Phase 2 · Arm & Deliver. With the target list in hand, they build a weapon and pick a delivery ride.
The #1 ride? Email. Walk through the three steps to craft a malicious doc and phish poor Mike.
Phase 3 · Exploitation. Mike opens what LOOKS like an invoice. He sees a warning. Just one careless click…
Victim POV: Click the glowing Enable Content button yourself to see exactly what fires behind the scenes.
Phase 4 · Establish Control. Code is running on Mike's machine. The attacker plants backdoors and calls home.
Run each step in order to watch persistence get installed, then see the encrypted C2 tunnel come alive.
Phase 5 · Exfiltration. Mission-objective time — steal the crown jewels. This is the loud part.
Click files one at a time, or smash-and-grab everything at once. Either way, the damage is done.
Real-world time! This is MGM Resorts, September 2023. Scattered Spider used every phase you just learned, in exact order.
A single 10-minute phone call to the IT help desk caused over $100M in losses. Social engineering is scary powerful!
When you're ready, hit Start the Quiz to prove what you learned!

steps.title: LinkedIn & Social Profiling
steps.desc: Attackers profiled an MGM IT staffer by scraping LinkedIn and other social media — his role, his colleagues, even his speaking style.

steps.title: The 10-Minute Phone Call
steps.desc: They called the IT help desk impersonating the employee and convinced the agent to reset his credentials — no malware needed.

steps.title: Identity Provider Takeover
steps.desc: With the reset credential, they logged into MGM's Okta tenant and escalated privileges across the identity platform.

steps.title: Lateral Movement & Persistence
steps.desc: They moved from cloud identity into on-prem Active Directory, installing a secondary identity provider for persistent access.

steps.title: Ransomware & $100M in Losses
steps.desc: BlackCat ransomware was deployed across thousands of ESXi hosts; slot machines, room keys, and reservations went dark for 10 days.

challenges.label: Challenge 1
challenges.title: Plan the Breach
challenges.desc: You're the attacker now. Make five decisions — from OSINT to payload — to build a phishing operation against ACME Corp. Pick wrong and you get burned.

mascot_script.text: Time to switch sides, nyaa~! For this challenge you ARE the attacker.
Five decisions: target, pretext, channel, lure, payload. Each one you get right, the operation survives. Each one you get wrong, the defenders catch you.
Think like a red-teamer. Who's the easiest mark? What story would THEY fall for? What payload bypasses 2024 defenses? Let's plan this breach!

steps.label: Target
steps.heading: Pick Your Mark
steps.prompt: You've scraped LinkedIn, breach dumps, and public posts for four ACME employees. Who has the highest attacker value?

options.title: Carol Lee
options.subtitle: Security Analyst
chips: Posts detection engineering tips on Twitter weekly
options.rationale: Carol literally teaches Mike how to spot you. She'll read the email headers, sandbox the attachment, and have your C2 domain reported before lunch. Hard pass.

options.title: Mike Smith
options.subtitle: IT Administrator
chips: Local-admin on 200+ endpoints (per his job post)
Email appears in 2021 LinkedIn breach dump — 87% of users reuse
Tweeted yesterday: 'drowning in tickets, someone save me'
options.rationale: Jackpot. Admin privileges turn a foothold into domain dominance. His password is in a public dump, so he probably reuses it. And he's overloaded — distracted people click fast.

options.title: Jane Doe
options.subtitle: Chief Financial Officer
chips: Listed on ACME's public About page
Standard user privileges — no admin rights
options.rationale: Great for Business Email Compromise, but she can't install anything. You'd land in a fancy inbox with nowhere to pivot. Wrong tool for a foothold-first op.

options.title: Bob Johnson
options.subtitle: Software Engineer
chips: LinkedIn updated: 'Open to work' — left ACME 2 weeks ago
Email auto-reply: 'I am no longer with ACME'
options.rationale: Dead account. His mail bounces, his badge is deactivated, and his old endpoint was wiped. You'd be phishing a ghost.

steps.label: Pretext
steps.heading: Craft the Pretext
steps.prompt: Mike's an overloaded sysadmin. Which backstory would actually grab HIS attention?

options.title: Mandatory CEO all-hands survey
options.subtitle: From: CEO · Feedback request
options.rationale: Mike doesn't care about all-hands fluff. He'll archive it in 0.2 seconds, like every other sysadmin on earth.

options.title: Crowdstrike Falcon license renewal — URGENT
options.subtitle: From: Crowdstrike Billing · DocuSign
options.rationale: Perfect. Crowdstrike is the EDR Mike maintains; a lapsed license = executives screaming = tickets = his exact pain. He'll click without reading headers.

options.title: Overdue vendor invoice — please remit
options.subtitle: From: Accounts Payable
options.rationale: Mike doesn't handle accounts payable. He'd forward this to finance and keep ticket-crunching. Zero engagement.

options.title: Happy Birthday from ACME HR
options.subtitle: From: HR · Cheer
options.rationale: Cute. Useless. There's nothing to click, nothing urgent, nothing he'd even read past the subject.

steps.label: Channel
steps.heading: Pick the Delivery Channel
steps.prompt: How do you get a 'Crowdstrike renewal' in front of Mike without tripping alarms?

options.title: Spoofed corporate email
options.subtitle: billing@crowdstrlke.com (typo-squat)
options.rationale: Vendor billing lives in email. The typo-squat domain fools casual scanning, and the pretext matches the channel's expected traffic. Defensive filters exist but are beatable with a warmed-up sending domain.

options.title: SMS to his personal cell
options.subtitle: Harvested via Apollo.io lookup
chips: But: vendor billing over SMS is bizarre
options.rationale: No legit SaaS vendor texts license renewals to personal cells. Mike would smell it immediately and forward to Carol.

options.title: LinkedIn InMail
options.subtitle: From 'Crowdstrike Account Exec' profile
options.rationale: LinkedIn isn't where renewals happen. The platform flags unknown-contact messages, and Mike's not checking InMail while drowning in tickets.

options.title: Slack DM via compromised integration
options.subtitle: From a breached third-party bot
options.rationale: This is the dream delivery — but it needs an existing compromise of a vendor or integration. You don't have one. That's what the whole op is FOR.

steps.label: Lure
steps.heading: Weaponize the Lure
steps.prompt: You've got the inbox. Now write the email. Which subject line + body gets Mike to click?

options.title: "Hello friend, please open invoice.pdf"
options.subtitle: Bare, grammatically-broken body. No branding.
options.rationale: This is bait for your grandma, not a sysadmin. Proofpoint eats it for breakfast and Mike laughs at it in the quarantine report.

options.title: "Q1 performance review — action required"
options.subtitle: HR-style body, no vendor branding.
chips: HR tone for an IT target
options.rationale: You committed to the Crowdstrike pretext two steps ago. Now you're sending an HR email? Mike sees the mismatch and flags it.

options.title: "ACTION REQUIRED: Falcon sensor license expires today — DocuSign"
options.subtitle: Branded Crowdstrike template, DocuSign button, countdown timer.
options.rationale: Consistency + urgency + branding. The subject line names Mike's exact product and tool. The countdown creates panic. The single DocuSign CTA removes decision friction. He'll click in under 10 seconds.

options.title: "Your package will be delivered tomorrow"
options.subtitle: FedEx-style tracking email.
options.rationale: Package-delivery phishing is for consumer mass-campaigns, not targeted ops. And you already committed to Crowdstrike — stay in character.

steps.label: Payload
steps.heading: Arm the Payload
steps.prompt: DocuSign button clicked. Now — what fires? Pick the payload that survives modern defenses.

options.title: .docm macro-enabled invoice
options.subtitle: Classic Office macro → PowerShell dropper
options.rationale: Microsoft disabled internet-origin macros by default in 2022. Your payload dies at the 'Enable Content' wall that almost nobody sees anymore. Outdated.

options.title: HTML smuggling → ISO → LNK → stager
options.subtitle: Embed blob in HTML, user mounts ISO, LNK runs script
chips: Used by APT29, Qakbot, Emotet in 2023–24
options.rationale: This is the 2024 red-team playbook. HTML smuggling sneaks the payload past perimeter scanners. ISO mounting detaches the Mark-of-the-Web so Windows doesn't warn. The LNK fires a PowerShell stager that lives off the land. Beautiful.

options.title: .exe renamed to .pdf
options.subtitle: Double-extension trick from 2005
options.rationale: No mail gateway on earth lets a bare .exe through anymore. This is the cybersecurity equivalent of showing up to a gunfight with a slingshot.

options.title: Chrome zero-day drive-by RCE
options.subtitle: 0-day browser exploit from a gray-market broker
options.rationale: Yes, it'd pop Mike's browser instantly. Also: you just spent a million-dollar zero-day on a mid-size company. Real operators save 0-days for targets worth nation-state budgets.

challenges.label: Challenge 2
challenges.title: Defend the Network
challenges.desc: Now flip sides. An attack is unfolding in real-time and you're the blue team. At each phase, pick the correct defensive action to stop the attacker before they reach their objective.

mascot_script.text: Final challenge! You just played the attacker — now you're on the blue team. An attack is unfolding and you have to pick the right defense at each phase.
Hit Start Simulation when you're ready. Five phases, one shot each — no pressure, nyaa~!

rounds.alert: An unknown entity is scanning your public infrastructure and scraping employee data from social media profiles.

options.text: Limit public exposure & deploy social engineering awareness training
Patch all known CVEs on internal application servers
Deploy DLP tools to monitor outbound data transfers

rounds.alert: A wave of spoofed emails carrying suspicious attachments is flooding employee inboxes, disguised as urgent invoices from a trusted vendor.

options.text: Monitor internal network for unusual lateral movement
Deploy email gateway filtering & sandbox suspicious attachments
Rotate all employee account passwords immediately

rounds.alert: An employee opened a document that is exploiting an unpatched vulnerability to execute malicious code on their workstation.

options.text: Patch known vulnerabilities & enforce macro-disabled policies

rounds.alert: A compromised workstation is making regular encrypted connections to an unknown overseas IP every 30 seconds.

options.text: Isolate the endpoint & block C2 traffic at the firewall
Conduct a vulnerability scan on public-facing web apps

rounds.alert: Anomalous data flows detected: 2.4 GB of compressed files being transferred to an external IP address during off-hours.

options.text: Enable multi-factor authentication across all accounts
Implement DLP monitoring & block unauthorized bulk transfers
Deploy an updated email security gateway
